---
title: "Inspecting, Selecting, and Cleaning"
description: "Understand DataFrames, choose relevant data, and handle missing values."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Understand DataFrames, choose relevant data, and handle missing values.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Inspecting, Selecting, and Cleaning** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 3 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Inspecting DataFrames** — Use shape, columns, dtypes, head, info, and describe to understand data.
- Section 2: **Selecting and Filtering Data** — Choose columns, rows, and conditions from a DataFrame.
- Section 3: **Cleaning Missing Values** — Find and handle missing data deliberately.

## Section 1: Inspecting DataFrames

Use shape, columns, dtypes, head, info, and describe to understand data.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Inspection is checking a shipment before unpacking everything: count boxes, read labels,
notice damage.
:::

### Mental model

Inspection reveals structure before you transform or model data.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [1, 2, 3], "group": ["a", "a", "b"]})
print(df.shape)
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.describe())
```

### Walkthrough

1. `import pandas as pd` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [1, 2, 3], "group": ["a", "a", "b"]})` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print(df.shape)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(df.dtypes)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
5. `print(df.describe())` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Assuming column types from appearance is risky. Always inspect dtypes.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-science-inspecting-dataframes",
  "title": "Inspecting DataFrames checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Inspecting DataFrames?",
      "options": [
        "Inspection reveals structure before you transform or model data.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Inspection reveals structure before you transform or model data."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Inspection is checking a shipment before unpacking everything: count boxes, read labels, notice damage."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: Selecting and Filtering Data

Choose columns, rows, and conditions from a DataFrame.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Filtering is like using a sieve: keep rows that match the condition and let others pass
away.
:::

### Mental model

Selection narrows a table to relevant columns or rows.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"name": ["Ada", "Grace"], "score": [95, 88]})
high = df[df["score"] >= 90]
print(high[["name", "score"]])
```

### Walkthrough

1. `import pandas as pd` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `df = pd.DataFrame({"name": ["Ada", "Grace"], "score": [95, 88]})` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `high = df[df["score"] >= 90]` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(high[["name", "score"]])` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Use parentheses around combined conditions in pandas: (a) & (b), not a and b.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-science-selecting-filtering-data",
  "title": "Selecting and Filtering Data checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Selecting and Filtering Data?",
      "options": [
        "Selection narrows a table to relevant columns or rows.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Selection narrows a table to relevant columns or rows."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Filtering is like using a sieve: keep rows that match the condition and let others pass away."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 3: Cleaning Missing Values

Find and handle missing data deliberately.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Missing values are blank cells on a form: you must decide whether to ask again, fill
carefully, or exclude.
:::

### Mental model

Missing data are absent values that can affect calculations and conclusions.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [10, None, 30]})
print(df.isna().sum())
print(df.dropna())
```

### Walkthrough

1. `import pandas as pd` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [10, None, 30]})` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print(df.isna().sum())` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(df.dropna())` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Dropping missing rows can bias results. Explain your cleaning decision.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-science-cleaning-missing-values",
  "title": "Cleaning Missing Values checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Cleaning Missing Values?",
      "options": [
        "Missing data are absent values that can affect calculations and conclusions.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Missing data are absent values that can affect calculations and conclusions."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Missing values are blank cells on a form: you must decide whether to ask again, fill carefully, or exclude."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Inspecting DataFrames**: Inspection reveals structure before you transform or model data.
- **Selecting and Filtering Data**: Selection narrows a table to relevant columns or rows.
- **Cleaning Missing Values**: Missing data are absent values that can affect calculations and conclusions.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- pandas documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/
- seaborn documentation: https://seaborn.pydata.org/
- Matplotlib documentation: https://matplotlib.org/stable/
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
